text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 18 August 2019 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Thomas Reynolds, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH784512 NHS No. 9876543201  
12 Hawthorn Close, Bromley, BR2 8JT

Thank you for referring your patient back to clinic. I reviewed him today following a period of escalating functional strain at work and concern from his partner that his overall stability is deteriorating. He works in a care setting with irregular breaks and frequent patient transfers. Over the past two months he and his supervisor report cumulative fatigue with perceived worsening of his day-to-day resilience, increasing recovery times after shifts, and more frequent near-miss episodes of confusion on returning home. He describes high physical demand and disrupted sleep due to early starts and late finishes.

He came off Levetiracetam on 18-Jul. In the following week, he had 4 seizures, one associated with sleep deprivation. No further seizures have occurred since. This sequence has been corroborated by his partner and workplace incident logs. Although there have been no additional witnessed events after that first week, he and his partner both feel that his premonitory symptoms (brief disorientation, difficulty focusing, and a sense of "pressure behind the eyes") have become more frequent, particularly after double shifts. He denies any new injuries but notes prolonged post-event fatigue compared with earlier in the year.

Interictally, he reports increasing irritability and reduced concentration at work, with supervisors expressing concern about his slowing and forgetfulness during manual handling tasks. He remains keen to continue his role but acknowledges that the current pattern of irregular breaks and repeated patient transfers is taking its toll. He is not driving. There is no history of tongue biting or incontinence during the recent events, and his partner has not observed clearly lateralised features. There have been no sustained nocturnal events reported in the past fortnight, though his sleep remains fragmented.

On examination today he was tired but cooperative, oriented, with halting recall over complex sequences and mild psychomotor slowing. Cranial nerves, tone, power, and coordination were unremarkable. Gait was steady. Cardiovascular observations were stable. There were no focal neurological signs.

Overall, despite the absence of further seizures since the week immediately following cessation of Levetiracetam, I am concerned that his condition is deteriorating in a broader functional sense, driven by cumulative fatigue, sleep disruption, and increased physical demand at work. The pattern he and his partner describe suggests reduced threshold for breakthrough events under sleep deprivation, and he is struggling more with recovery and day-to-day cognition after shifts.

I will arrange urgent scalp EEG with prolonged ambulatory recording to characterise current events and interictal activity. I have asked for updated bloods including FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, B12/folate, and vitamin D given fatigue. I have also requested an MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) as the last imaging was over three years ago. He has agreed to maintain a structured sleep and symptom diary, noting shift length, breaks taken, and any premonitory sensations.

Occupationally, he and his manager have already trialled fixed breaks on two shifts with some perceived benefit. He will discuss temporary modification of duties to reduce repetitive heavy transfers while we complete reassessment. He is aware to avoid lone working during prolonged shifts where possible.

I will review him in six weeks or sooner if there are further events. Thank you for your ongoing support and for facilitating workplace liaison. Please let me know if you would like any additional information.

Yours sincerely

He came off Levetiracetam on 18-Jul. In the following week, he had 4 seizures, one associated with sleep deprivation. No further seizures have occurred since

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 18 Aug 2019. He stopped Levetiracetam on 18-Jul. In the week following 18-Jul, he had 4 seizures; none occurred after that. He is not seizure free for ≥6 months (only ~1 month since last seizure), so we must calculate a frequency. Over the month from 18-Jul to 18-Aug, total seizures = 4, yielding 4 per month.
